one_line_takeaway: Taipan shows that multiple sensitive attributes can be jointly inferred from a publicly released graph alone, via transfer from an auxiliary graph and no victim-model interaction, and that this leakage substantially survives both node-level differential privacy and fairness-based graph debiasing.
key_finding: On same-distribution transfer (Table 2), Taipan generally outperforms both PreTr. and SingP. across most metrics and encoders/datasets, e.g., on German+GCN: AA 70.01 vs. PreTr. 66.34 vs. SingP. 66.50 vs. Random 54.21; SuA 57.00 vs. PreTr. 54.00 vs. SingP. 14.00 vs. Random 26.00. SingP. sometimes attains higher AA/AF (e.g., on Pokec-n+GraphSAGE, SingP. exceeds Taipan by 4.57% in AA) but shows much larger Task Deviation (TDA/TDF) and lower SuA/LC, i.e., unstable, unfair, single-attribute-dominated performance versus Taipan's more balanced joint inference. Under heterogeneous/OOD transfer (Table 4), Taipan still generally beats SingP./PreTr., though absolute performance drops further under larger distribution shift (Credit-to-German AA ~44-55 vs. Pokec-n-to-Pokec-z AA ~58-73). Ablations (Table 3) show pretext tokens contribute most to attack performance, followed by the attack hierarchy; removing all Taipan components drops AA from 70.01 to 52.44 on German+GCN. Node degree and homophily positively correlate with per-node subset accuracy — high-degree, homophilic-neighborhood nodes are most vulnerable. Under defenses (Table 5), strict node-DP (via PrivDPR, ε=0.1) fails to prevent the attack in dense/homophilic graphs like Credit (AA 51.06, close to no-defense) and only partially degrades performance elsewhere (e.g., Pokec-z AA remains 73.34 though AF/task-fairness worsen); the authors attribute large drops on Pokec datasets partly to poor synthetic-graph quality (downstream GNN utility falls to ~64/53/44% AUC/ACC/F1 on Pokec-n). Fairness-based defense (MAPPING) is consistently weaker than node-DP at suppressing the attack — attack performance on MAPPING-debiased graphs is higher than on DP-protected ones for both single- and joint-attribute inference, even as downstream GNN utility improves (e.g., Pokec-n AUC/ACC/F1 rises to 71.25/57.61/51.10%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Taipan, the first query-free, transfer-based attack framework for jointly inferring Multiple Sensitive Attributes on Graphs (G-MSAIAs), i.e., an attribute inference attack (AIA) that requires no interaction with a victim model, relying solely on publicly released auxiliary graphs. Taipan has two modules: (1) Hierarchical Attack Knowledge Routing, which profiles inter-attribute correlations on an auxiliary graph via agglomerative hierarchical clustering of pairwise cosine similarities among sensitive attributes, then builds a hierarchical Multi-gate Mixture-of-Experts (MMoE, extended to graphs as the authors' own GMMoE construction with shared, cluster-shared, and task-specific GNN experts plus gating networks) guided by learnable pretext tokens acting as task identifiers; and (2) Prompt-guided Attack Prototype Refinement, which adapts the frozen pre-trained model to an unlabeled target graph by tuning only lightweight pretext tokens, using pseudo-labeling with a confidence threshold (High-confidence Tuning) plus EMA-updated class prototypes and an entropy-based alignment loss for filtered/low-confidence nodes (Adaptive Prototype Adjustment). The paper also proposes a new evaluation suite for G-MSAIAs spanning confidence-based (Average AUC/F1, Task Deviation AUC/F1), correctness-based (Hamming Distance, Subset Accuracy), and semantics-based (Semantic Difference, Label Consistency, both via distance correlation/dCor) metrics.
threat_model: Data-only, query-free adversary with no access to the victim GNN's parameters, training procedure, internal representations, or query interface. The adversary knows only the possible values of the target graph's sensitive attributes (a standard assumption in prior AIA work) and has access to the target graph's non-sensitive attributes and topology, which may be privacy-processed (e.g., masked sensitive attributes, or protected by differential privacy at graph-publishing time). The adversary pre-trains an attack model Fpre on a labeled auxiliary graph Ga (which may be same-distribution, out-of-distribution, or task-shifted relative to the target) and transfers it to the unlabeled target graph Gt to infer sensitive attributes for a subset of target nodes. This is explicitly distinguished from conventional query-based AIAs (e.g., FJRMIA, CSMIA, LOMIA) that assume repeated probing of a deployed model, and from prior graph AIAs (Duddu et al., which needs node-embedding access, and FP/FP-MA, which are imputation methods for a single missing attribute) — the paper's Table 1 formally contrasts these along attack target/participant/knowledge/mechanism axes, with Taipan being the only 'All'-phase, multi-attribute, data-only, transfer-based entry.
limitations: The paper's own limitations/scope notes: (1) heterogeneous UDA across mismatched feature dimensionalities is handled only via a simple PCA projection, with the authors explicitly stating 'more advanced heterogeneous UDA techniques are left for future work'; (2) no direct empirical comparison to prior graph AIAs (Duddu et al., FP/FP-MA) is possible because their threat models (embedding access, repeated queries) are incompatible with Taipan's query-free setting, so superiority over prior graph attacks is argued qualitatively (Table 1) rather than benchmarked head-to-head; (3) attack performance is uneven across GNN encoders — GIN consistently underperforms GCN/GraphSAGE except on the dense Credit graph, attributed to GIN's sum-based aggregation being more sensitive to structural noise on low-average-degree graphs, and the paper notes pretext tokens' extra parameters may amplify this noise; (4) under node-DP, degraded attack performance on Pokec datasets is confounded with degraded downstream GNN utility from poor synthetic-graph quality, meaning the paper cannot cleanly separate 'DP protects against Taipan' from 'DP degrades the graph so much that all downstream tasks suffer'; (5) evaluation is limited to four datasets and two defenses (DP, one fairness method), both from existing tooling (PrivDPR, MAPPING) rather than defenses purpose-built to counter transfer-based data-only attacks.

quantitative_results: Same-distribution, German+GCN: Taipan AA 70.01 / AF 76.10 / SuA 57.00 vs. PreTr. AA 66.34 / AF 73.62 / SuA 54.00 vs. SingP. AA 66.50 / AF 42.50 / SuA 14.00 vs. Random AA 54.21 / SuA 26.00
Ablation on German+GCN: removing all Taipan components (w.o.all) drops AA to 52.44 and AF to 61.94, vs. full Taipan AA 70.01 / AF 76.10
Heterogeneous OOD transfer Pokec-n->Pokec-z, GCN: Taipan AA 64.33 vs. PreTr. 62.22 vs. SingP. 71.37 (SingP. higher AA but larger TDA 35.58 vs. Taipan's 23.66)
Heterogeneous transfer Credit->German, GCN: Taipan AA 50.75 / SuA 44.00 vs. SingP. AA 44.25 / SuA 33.00
Node-DP defense (PrivDPR, (0.1,1e-5)-node-DP) on Credit: Taipan AA 51.06, close to no-defense performance, showing DP fails to protect dense/homophilic graphs
Node-DP defense on Pokec-n synthetic graphs: downstream GNN utility only 64.07% AUC / 52.84% ACC / 43.65% F1
MAPPING fairness defense on Pokec-n: downstream GNN utility improves to 71.25% AUC / 57.61% ACC / 51.10% F1, while attack performance remains higher than under node-DP
baselines_compared: Single Prediction (SingP., per-attribute models, hierarchy/transfer ablation)
Pre-training (PreTr., pre-trained Taipan without Prompt-guided Attack Prototype Refinement)
